title: Testing
description: Unit tests, UI tests, testing coroutines, screenshot testing, and the testing pyramid
lesson:
The Testing Pyramid

Android testing follows a pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewest UI tests. Each level has different tradeoffs:

/ UI / E2E \ ~10% — Slowest, flakiest, highest confidence / \ Tests real user journeys end-to-end / Integration \ ~20% — Medium speed, real components / \ Tests component interactions / Unit Tests \ ~70% — Fastest, most reliable / \ Tests single class in isolation ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
Cost / Speed / Confidence Tradeoffs: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Speed Cost Confidence Flakiness ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Unit ~ms Low Low-Medium Very Low Integration ~sec Medium Medium-High Low E2E / UI ~min High Highest High ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Rule: Write MANY fast unit tests, FEW slow UI tests. Unit tests catch most bugs. UI tests catch integration issues.
Key insight: Unit tests are cheap and fast — write many. UI tests are expensive and flaky — write few but covering critical user journeys. Integration tests fill the gap, testing real component interactions without full UI.

Test Doubles: Fake, Mock, Stub, Spy

Test doubles replace real dependencies in tests. Each serves a different purpose:

Test Double Types: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ FAKE Working implementation with shortcuts Example: FakeUserRepository backed by HashMap When: Most tests. Google's recommended default. STUB Returns hardcoded values, no logic Example: always returns User("Alice") When: You only care about the return value, not behavior. MOCK Records interactions for verification Example: verify(repo).save(user) was called When: You need to verify a method WAS called. Library: Mockito, MockK SPY Wraps a real object, overrides specific methods Example: real repo but overridden network call When: You need mostly real behavior with one override. ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Preference order: Fake > Stub > Mock > Spy Fakes test behavior. Mocks test implementation details.
// Fake — has real logic, uses in-memory data class FakeUserRepository : UserRepository { private val users = mutableMapOf<String, User>() override suspend fun getUser(id: String): User? = users[id] override suspend fun saveUser(user: User) { users[user.id] = user } override suspend fun deleteUser(id: String) { users.remove(id) } // Test helpers fun seedUsers(vararg userList: User) { userList.forEach { users[it.id] = it } } fun clear() { users.clear() } }
Why Google recommends fakes over mocks: Mocks verify HOW code works (implementation details) — refactoring breaks tests even if behavior is correct. Fakes verify WHAT code does (behavior) — tests survive refactoring. Mocks also make tests harder to read and maintain.

ViewModel Testing

class UserViewModelTest { // Replace Dispatchers.Main (doesn't exist in JVM tests) private val testDispatcher = UnconfinedTestDispatcher() @Before fun setup() { Dispatchers.setMain(testDispatcher) } @After fun tearDown() { Dispatchers.resetMain() } @Test fun loadUsers_emitsLoadingThenSuccess() = runTest { val fakeRepo = FakeUserRepository() fakeRepo.seedUsers(User("1", "Alice"), User("2", "Bob")) val viewModel = UserViewModel(fakeRepo) // Test StateFlow emissions with Turbine viewModel.uiState.test { assertEquals(UiState.Loading, awaitItem()) viewModel.loadUsers() val success = awaitItem() assertTrue(success is UiState.Success) assertEquals(2, (success as UiState.Success).users.size) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } @Test fun loadUsers_emitsErrorOnFailure() = runTest { val fakeRepo = FakeUserRepository().apply { shouldThrow = true // configure fake to fail } val viewModel = UserViewModel(fakeRepo) viewModel.loadUsers() assertTrue(viewModel.uiState.value is UiState.Error) } }

Coroutine Testing Deep Dive

// runTest: the coroutine test harness @Test fun testWithDelay() = runTest { var result = 0 launch { delay(1000) // virtual time — doesn't actually wait! result = 42 } advanceTimeBy(1000) // fast-forward virtual clock assertEquals(42, result) }
StandardTestDispatcher vs UnconfinedTestDispatcher: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ StandardTestDispatcher UnconfinedTestDispatcher ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Coroutines DON'T run Coroutines run EAGERLY automatically (immediately, no waiting) You control execution: Simpler for most tests advanceUntilIdle() No need to advance time advanceTimeBy(ms) runCurrent() Best for: testing timing, Best for: ViewModel tests delays, debounce, precise where you just want to coroutine ordering verify final state ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
// StandardTestDispatcher — precise control @Test fun testDebounce() = runTest { val dispatcher = StandardTestDispatcher(testScheduler) val viewModel = SearchViewModel(FakeRepo(), dispatcher) viewModel.onQueryChanged("kot") advanceTimeBy(100) // not enough for 300ms debounce assertTrue(viewModel.results.value.isEmpty()) // nothing yet advanceTimeBy(200) // now 300ms total assertTrue(viewModel.results.value.isNotEmpty()) // debounce fired! }

Repository Testing

// Test with in-memory Room database class ArticleRepositoryTest { private lateinit var db: AppDatabase private lateinit var dao: ArticleDao @Before fun setup() { db = Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder( ApplicationProvider.getApplicationContext(), AppDatabase::class.java ).allowMainThreadQueries().build() dao = db.articleDao() } @After fun tearDown() { db.close() } @Test fun insertAndQuery() = runTest { val article = Article(1, "Test", "Body") dao.insert(article) val result = dao.getById(1) assertEquals(article, result) } } // Test with MockWebServer (OkHttp) class UserApiTest { private val mockServer = MockWebServer() private lateinit var api: UserApi @Before fun setup() { mockServer.start() api = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl(mockServer.url("/")) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() .create(UserApi::class.java) } @After fun tearDown() { mockServer.shutdown() } @Test fun getUser_returnsUser() = runTest { mockServer.enqueue( MockResponse() .setBody("""{ "id": "1", "name": "Alice" }""") .setResponseCode(200) ) val user = api.getUser("1") assertEquals("Alice", user.name) // Verify the request was correct val request = mockServer.takeRequest() assertEquals("/users/1", request.path) } @Test fun getUser_handles404() = runTest { mockServer.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(404)) assertThrows<HttpException> { api.getUser("999") } } }

UI Testing

Compose Test APIs

// Compose UI testing class LoginScreenTest { @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @Test fun loginFlow_showsErrorOnInvalidEmail() { composeTestRule.setContent { LoginScreen(onLogin = {}) } // Find nodes by text, tag, content description composeTestRule .onNodeWithTag("email_field") .performTextInput("not-an-email") composeTestRule .onNodeWithText("Login") .performClick() // Assert error message appears composeTestRule .onNodeWithText("Invalid email") .assertIsDisplayed() } @Test fun loginFlow_successNavigates() { var navigated = false composeTestRule.setContent { LoginScreen(onLogin = { navigated = true }) } composeTestRule.onNodeWithTag("email_field").performTextInput("alice@test.com") composeTestRule.onNodeWithTag("password_field").performTextInput("password123") composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Login").performClick() assertTrue(navigated) } }

Espresso (View-based UI testing)

// Espresso — for View-based UIs @Test fun loginButton_isDisplayed() { // Find (Matcher) → Act (Action) → Assert (Assertion) onView(withId(R.id.btn_login)) // find by ID .check(matches(isDisplayed())) // assert visible onView(withId(R.id.et_email)) // find email field .perform(typeText("alice@test.com")) // type text onView(withId(R.id.btn_login)) // find button .perform(click()) // click it onView(withText("Welcome")) // find by text .check(matches(isDisplayed())) // assert visible } // Espresso pattern: onView(MATCHER).perform(ACTION).check(ASSERTION)

Flow Testing with Turbine

// Turbine provides .test { } for Flow assertions @Test fun searchFlow_emitsResultsAfterDebounce() = runTest { val viewModel = SearchViewModel(FakeSearchRepo()) viewModel.results.test { viewModel.onQueryChanged("kot") viewModel.onQueryChanged("kotl") viewModel.onQueryChanged("kotlin") advanceTimeBy(500) val emission = awaitItem() assertEquals(listOf("Kotlin", "Kotlin Coroutines"), emission) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } // Testing error flows @Test fun errorFlow_emitsException() = runTest { val errorFlow = flow<String> { throw IOException("fail") } errorFlow.test { val error = awaitError() assertTrue(error is IOException) } } // Testing StateFlow in ViewModel @Test fun viewModel_stateTransitions() = runTest { val viewModel = UserViewModel(FakeRepo()) viewModel.uiState.test { assertEquals(UiState.Initial, awaitItem()) // initial viewModel.loadData() assertEquals(UiState.Loading, awaitItem()) // loading assertEquals(UiState.Success(data), awaitItem()) // success cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } }

Screenshot Testing

Catch visual regressions by comparing screenshots pixel-by-pixel:

ToolRuns onSpeedSetup
RoborazziJVM (Robolectric)Fast — no emulatorSimple Gradle plugin
PaparazziJVM (layoutlib)Fast — no emulatorSquare's library
Compose Preview ScreenshotJVMFastGoogle's official tool
// Roborazzi screenshot test @RunWith(RobolectricTestRunner::class) class ProfileScreenTest { @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @Test fun profileScreen_default() { composeTestRule.setContent { ProfileScreen(user = testUser) } composeTestRule.onRoot().captureRoboImage() } } // Paparazzi screenshot test class ButtonScreenshotTest { @get:Rule val paparazzi = Paparazzi( deviceConfig = DeviceConfig.PIXEL_5, theme = "android:Theme.Material.Light" ) @Test fun primaryButton() { paparazzi.snapshot { PrimaryButton(text = "Submit", onClick = {}) } } } // First run: creates golden image // Subsequent runs: compares against golden, fails on diff // Update goldens: ./gradlew recordPaparazzi (or recordRoborazzi)

Robolectric vs Instrumented Tests

RobolectricInstrumented
Runs on JVM — fast (seconds)Runs on device/emulator — slow (minutes)
Simulates Android frameworkReal Android framework
May have fidelity gaps100% fidelity
Great for: logic, simple UINeeded for: hardware, sensors, complex UI

Testing with Hilt

@HiltAndroidTest @UninstallModules(RepositoryModule::class) class SearchScreenTest { @get:Rule val hiltRule = HiltAndroidRule(this) @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @BindValue val fakeRepo: UserRepository = FakeUserRepository() @Before fun setup() { hiltRule.inject() } @Test fun showsResults() { composeTestRule.setContent { SearchScreen() } composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Results").assertExists() } }

questions.title: Compose UI Test
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to write a Compose UI test.
questions.explanation: createComposeRule() creates a test environment. onNodeWithText() finds by text. assertExists() verifies presence. onNode(hasText()) uses matchers. performClick() simulates user input.
hints: The test rule creates a Compose testing environment

questions.title: Testing Coroutines
questions.description: This ViewModel test is flaky — sometimes passes, sometimes fails. Fix the coroutine testing setup.

checks.message: Use runTest and set up a TestDispatcher

questions.explanation: Coroutines in viewModelScope use Dispatchers.Main, which doesn't exist in unit tests. Fix: Dispatchers.setMain(testDispatcher) replaces Main. runTest properly advances coroutine execution. Always reset in @After.
hints: viewModelScope uses Dispatchers.Main — does that exist in a JVM test?

questions.title: Test Doubles
questions.description: Which test double provides a simplified but working implementation?
options: Mock — records interactions
Stub — returns hardcoded values
Fake — has a working implementation (e.g., in-memory database)
Spy — wraps a real object
questions.explanation: A Fake is a simplified but functional implementation. Example: FakeUserRepository using a HashMap instead of a real database. Google recommends fakes over mocks for most Android testing.
hints: Which test double actually has working logic?

questions.title: ViewModel Unit Test
questions.description: Write a unit test for a ViewModel that tests the loading state flow: initial Loading → Success with data.
questions.explanation: Key: test dispatcher replaces Dispatchers.Main, fake repo provides data, runTest handles coroutines, assert against StateFlow value.
hints: You need three things: test dispatcher, fake repository, and runTest

questions.title: Turbine Flow Testing
questions.description: What does this test verify using the Turbine library?
options: It verifies that every keystroke triggers a search
It verifies that the search debounces — only the last query ("kotlin") produces results after 500ms
It verifies that the search filters out short queries
It verifies that search results are cached
questions.explanation: Turbine is a testing library for Kotlin Flows. .test { } collects emissions. Three queries are sent rapidly, then advanceTimeBy(500) simulates the debounce delay. Only one emission is expected — the results for "kotlin". This confirms the debounce works: intermediate queries are ignored, only the final one triggers a search after the delay.
hints: advanceTimeBy simulates time passing — why is that needed for debounce?
Only one awaitItem() is called — meaning only one emission is expected

questions.title: Fake Repository
questions.description: Write a FakeUserRepository that implements UserRepository interface. It should store users in a mutable list and support getAll(), insert(), and clear() operations.

checks.message: Store users in an in-memory list

questions.explanation: A fake uses real data structures (in-memory list) instead of a real database. toList() returns a defensive copy. Extra methods like setUsers() and clear() make test setup easy. This is simpler and more reliable than mocking.
hints: Use a MutableList as the backing store
Add helper methods for test setup convenience

questions.title: Testing Pyramid
questions.description: According to the testing pyramid, which type of test should make up the majority of your test suite?
options: UI / end-to-end tests — they give the most confidence
Integration tests — they balance speed and confidence
Unit tests — they are fast, isolated, and deterministic
Manual tests — automated tests can't catch everything
questions.explanation: The testing pyramid recommends ~70% unit tests, ~20% integration, ~10% UI tests. Unit tests are fast (milliseconds), run on JVM, and test logic in isolation. They're cheap to write and maintain. UI tests give high confidence but are slow and flaky. A healthy test suite has many fast unit tests and few targeted UI tests for critical flows.
hints: Which test type is fastest and cheapest to maintain?

questions.title: Screenshot Testing Tools
questions.description: Which screenshot testing tool runs entirely on the JVM (no emulator needed) and uses Robolectric's native rendering?
options: Espresso Screenshot
Roborazzi — JVM-based screenshot capture using Robolectric
Firebase Test Lab — cloud device screenshots
Layout Inspector — manual visual comparison
questions.explanation: Roborazzi captures screenshots on the JVM using Robolectric's rendering engine. No emulator or device needed — tests run in seconds. It compares against golden images and fails if there are visual differences beyond a threshold. Paparazzi (by Square) is similar but uses Android's layoutlib directly. Both are fast CI-friendly alternatives to device-based screenshot tests.
hints: The fastest screenshot tests don't need a device at all

questions.title: Robolectric vs Instrumented
questions.description: When should you prefer instrumented tests over Robolectric?
options: Always — Robolectric is outdated and unreliable
When you need 100% fidelity with real Android behavior, such as testing hardware sensors, complex animations, or multi-process scenarios
For all ViewModel tests to ensure accuracy
When testing pure Kotlin logic with no Android dependencies
questions.explanation: Robolectric simulates the Android framework on JVM — it's fast but may have fidelity gaps for advanced features. Use instrumented tests when you need the real Android framework: hardware sensors, camera, complex touch gestures, multi-process IPC, or behavior that differs across API levels. For ViewModels, Repositories, and simple UI, Robolectric is faster and sufficient.
hints: Robolectric simulates Android — some edge cases behave differently

questions.title: Hilt Test Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up a Hilt-enabled test that replaces the real repository with a fake.

blanks.hint: Marks a test class for Hilt injection
Removes the real module so you can replace it
Binds this field directly into the Hilt graph

questions.explanation: @HiltAndroidTest enables Hilt in tests. @UninstallModules removes the production module that provides UserRepository. @BindValue injects the fake directly into the graph — no module needed. hiltRule.inject() triggers injection. This pattern lets you test with controlled data without mocking frameworks.
hints: Three annotations set up the replacement: test marker, uninstall, and bind
@BindValue is simpler than creating a test module

questions.title: Screenshot Test
questions.description: Write a Roborazzi screenshot test that captures the UserCard composable in both light and dark themes.
questions.explanation: Screenshot tests render a composable and capture an image. On first run, it creates golden images. On subsequent runs, it compares against goldens and fails if there's a visual difference. Testing both themes catches color/contrast regressions. captureRoboImage() is Roborazzi's API — Paparazzi uses paparazzi.snapshot { }.
hints: Wrap the composable in your theme with darkTheme parameter
captureRoboImage saves/compares against a golden image file
